item.title: 【How to Get the Most Out of KubeCon + CloudNativeCon Europe 2026】如何充分利用 KubeCon + CloudNativeCon Europe 2026
item.description:
【

KubeCon + CloudNativeCon Europe is almost here again, and if you’re heading to Amsterdam in March 2026, you’re probably feeling a mix of excitement and mild panic. Thousands of people. Hundreds of sessions. Endless hallway conversations, and never quite enough energy.

I’ve attended KubeCon + CloudNativeCon a few times, and one thing hasn’t changed: the conference gives you exactly what you prepare for.


This guide is meant to help you plan ahead, be intentional with your time, and ultimately get the most out of your KubeCon + CloudNativeCon Europe 2026 experience.

Part 1: Understanding the KubeCon landscape

Before jumping into schedules, tips, and endurance strategies, it helps to understand how KubeCon + CloudNativeCon is actually structured. The event is more than just “talks”; it’s a full week with different formats, spaces, and opportunities, each serving a different purpose.

Knowing what these are (and what they’re for) makes planning your time much easier.

Co-located events (Monday)

KubeCon + CloudNativeCon week officially starts on Monday, dedicated entirely to co-located events. While the main conference runs from Tuesday to Thursday, Monday is reserved for focused, topic-specific events such as Platform Engineering Day, ArgoCon, CiliumCon, Kubernetes on Edge Day, and many more.

An important thing to know is that CNCF hosted co-located events require an All-Access pass.

These events usually go much deeper into a single area compared to regular KubeCon + CloudNativeCon talks and attract a more targeted audience. If you already know the domain you care about, Monday can easily become one of the most valuable days of the week.

Keynotes

Each conference day starts with keynotes, held in the largest venue space, where everyone gathers in one place. This is one of the few moments during KubeCon + CloudNativeCon when the entire audience is together.

Keynotes usually cover:

  • The state of cloud native and CNCF projects
  • Industry trends and big-picture direction
  • Community updates and major announcements

Beyond the content, the energy of the keynote sessions is special, with thousands of people, shared excitement, and a strong sense of community. After the keynotes, the conference splits into multiple parallel tracks across different rooms for the rest of the day.

Breakout sessions

After keynotes, talks run simultaneously across many rooms, covering a wide range of topics and experience levels. This is where choice overload often kicks in.

You’ll see:

  • Deep technical talks
  • Case studies from real-world production environments
  • Beginner, intermediate, and advanced sessions

Because sessions overlap, you’ll never be able to attend everything, and that’s normal. Understanding this early helps you make more intentional choices later.

Solution Showcase and Project Pavilion

A major part of KubeCon + CloudNativeCon happens outside the session rooms.

The Solution Showcase is where vendors and companies demonstrate their products and platforms, while the Project Pavilion is dedicated to CNCF and community-led open-source projects.

This area is especially valuable if:

  • You want to explore tools you keep hearing about
  • You’re evaluating technologies for your platform or team
  • You want to talk directly to maintainers or contributors

Visiting booths takes time and energy, so if this matters to you, make sure to explicitly reserve time for it in your schedule instead of treating it as an afterthought.

ContribFest

If you’re curious about contributing to open source but don’t know where to start, ContribFest is a great entry point.

These sessions are designed to:

  • Introduce CNCF projects and their contribution models
  • Help newcomers take their first steps
  • Offer hands-on guidance from maintainers and experienced contributors

ContribFest sessions are listed in the official schedule, and usually, you don’t need prior contribution experience to join.

Networking nook

The Networking Nook is a dedicated space for casual, informal conversations. 

It’s a great place to:

  • Meet new faces
  • Start spontaneous conversations
  • Take a break from structured sessions

If traditional networking feels intimidating, this space tends to be more relaxed and approachable.

Peer group mentorship

Peer Group Mentorship sessions offer small-group mentorship with experienced contributors and maintainers from across the CNCF ecosystem.

These sessions are especially helpful if you:

  • Want career or open-source guidance
  • Are stuck on a technical or community question
  • Prefer focused conversations over large-group settings

Transition to part two

Now that you know how the different pieces of KubeCon + CloudNativeCon fit together, the next challenge is deciding how to balance the week.

In the next section, I’ll share practical advice and lessons learned from attending the event a few times, things I wish I’d known earlier, and what I now do differently.

1. Build your schedule before you arrive

One of the best things you can do for yourself before KubeCon + CloudNativeCon starts is to spend some time building your schedule in advance.

The official KubeCon + CloudNativeCon Europe schedule is available online, and you can log in to bookmark the sessions you plan to attend.

I strongly recommend doing this before the event week, not the night before. It helps you:

  • See where your interests naturally go
  • Avoid overbooking yourself
  • Be more intentional about what you attend (and what you skip)

Also, download Sched, the mobile app; it’s incredibly useful during the conference for easy access and finding room locations.

One important practical tip: Always pick at least two sessions for each time slot.
Rooms can fill up quickly, talks may be farther apart than expected, or you might decide last-minute to stay for a conversation. Having a backup option means you won’t waste time or feel stressed if Plan A doesn’t work out.

2. Decide why you’re going before you open the schedule

Before bookmarking talks or downloading the app, pause and answer one question:

What do I want to walk away with from KubeCon + CloudNativeCon 2026?

Some examples:

  • A clearer direction for your platform or internal Kubernetes roadmap
  • Deep dives into one or two technical areas (not ten)
  • Maintainer or contributor conversations
  • Career growth: visibility, job leads, or peer validation
  • Community energy and motivation (this one is underrated)

If you don’t define this upfront, the schedule will define it for you, and that usually leads to exhaustion, not insight.

3. Curate your schedule with intention

KubeCon + CloudNativeCon schedules are packed with options for all tech interests and expertise levels. In 2026, the themes are broader and more mature than ever: platform engineering, AI workloads, security, observability, cost, and real-world operations.

My rule of thumb:

  • Pick 1 primary theme you deeply care about
  • Pick 1 secondary theme you’re curious about
  • Ignore the rest without guilt

You don’t need to attend everything. Many talks will be recorded. What you can’t replay later are:

  • hallway conversations
  • post-talk questions
  • spontaneous “we tried this in prod, and it failed” stories

Those are where the real learning happens.

4. Use talks as conversation starters, not destinations

A mindset shift that changed KubeCon + CloudNativeCon for me:

Talks are not the main event. People are.

Instead of racing between rooms all day:

  • Stay after sessions and talk to speakers
  • Ask other attendees why they chose the same talk
  • Use one good talk to spark three meaningful conversations

You’ll remember the discussions far longer than the slide decks.

5. If you’re introverted, tired, or managing health: that’s okay

KubeCon + CloudNativeCon is often associated with high energy, visibility, and networking, but there are many ways to participate and make it meaningful in your own way.

You’re allowed to:

  • Skip sessions to rest
  • Have fewer but deeper conversations
  • Say no to evening events
  • Get some ‘you’ time, in the event’s quiet rooms and relaxation zones 

6. Define your “success criteria” before you leave

Before the last day ends, ask yourself:

  • Did I learn something I can apply at work?
  • Did I meet at least one person I want to stay in touch with?
  • Did I gain clarity, not just information?

If the answer is yes, KubeCon + CloudNativeCon worked.

Everything else is a bonus.

Final thoughts

With intention, boundaries, and curiosity, KubeCon + CloudNativeCon Europe 2026 can be: 

  • grounding
  • inspiring
  • and genuinely useful

I’ll be happy to meet you, and if you’re going, I hope you leave with fewer slides saved and more meaningful connections made. I also hope to see you at my Lightning Talk: “No Portal? No Problem! How We Scaled Our Platform With Just Terraform and ArgoCD”
Haven’t registered for KubeCon + CloudNativeCon yet? There’s still time! Register now.

】

KubeCon + CloudNativeCon Europe 即将再次到来，如果您将于 2026 年 3 月前往阿姆斯特丹，您可能会感到兴奋和轻微的恐慌。数千人。数百场会议。没完没了的走廊谈话，却永远没有足够的能量。

我参加过 KubeCon + CloudNativeCon 几次，但有一件事没有改变：会议为您提供了您所准备的内容。


本指南旨在帮助您提前计划、合理安排时间，并最终充分利用您的 KubeCon + CloudNativeCon Europe 2026 体验。

第 1 部分：了解 KubeCon 格局

在深入了解日程安排、技巧和耐力策略之前，了解 KubeCon + CloudNativeCon 的实际结构会有所帮助。此次活动不仅仅是“演讲”，更是一场“对话”。这整整一周都有不同的形式、空间和机会，每种都有不同的目的。

了解这些是什么（以及它们的用途）可以让您更轻松地规划时间。

同地举办活动（周一）

KubeCon + CloudNativeCon 周于周一正式开始，完全致力于同地活动。虽然主会议从周二到周四举行，但周一专门用于举办重点突出的特定主题活动，例如平台工程日、ArgoCon、CiliumCon、边缘日上的 Kubernetes 等等。

需要了解的重要一点是 CNCF 主办的同地举办的活动需要全通通行证。

与常规 KubeCon + CloudNativeCon 演讲相比，这些活动通常会更深入地探讨单个领域，并吸引更有针对性的受众。如果您已经知道您关心的域名，星期一很容易成为一周中最有价值的日子之一。

主题演讲

每个会议日都以主题演讲开始，在最大的会场空间举行，每个人都聚集在一个地方。这是 KubeCon + CloudNativeCon 期间所有观众聚集在一起的少数时刻之一。

主题演讲通常涵盖：

  • 云原生和 CNCF 项目的现状
  • 行业趋势和总体方向
  • 社区动态和重大公告

除了内容之外，主题演讲的活力也很特别，有数千人参与，共同兴奋，并具有强烈的社区意识。主题演讲结束后，会议在当天剩余的时间里分成多个平行的轨道，在不同的房间进行。

分组会议

主题演讲后s，演讲在多个房间同时进行，涵盖广泛的主题和经验水平。这就是选择过载经常发生的地方。

你会看到：

  • 深入的技术讲座
  • 来自真实生产环境的案例研究
  • 初级、中级和高级课程

由于会议重叠，您永远无法参加所有活动，这是正常的。尽早了解这一点有助于您以后做出更有意的选择。

解决方案展示和项目馆

KubeCon + CloudNativeCon 的主要部分发生在会议室外。

解决方案展示是供应商和公司展示其产品和平台的地方，而项目馆专门用于 CNCF 和社区主导的开源项目。

如果满足以下条件，该领域尤其有价值：

  • 您想要探索经常听说的工具
  • 您正在评估适合您的平台或团队的技术
  • 您想直接与维护者或贡献者交谈

参观展位需要时间和精力，因此如果这对您很重要，请确保在您的日程安排中明确为此预留时间，而不是将其视为事后的想法。

ContribFest

如果您对为开源做出贡献感到好奇，但不知道从哪里开始，ContribFest 是一个很好的切入点。

这些会议旨在：

  • 介绍 CNCF 项目及其贡献模型
  • 帮助新人迈出第一步
  • 由维护人员和经验丰富的贡献者提供实践指导

ContribFest 会议已在官方日程中列出，通常您无需具备贡献经验即可参加。

网络角落

社交角落是一个用于休闲、非正式对话的专用空间。 

这是一个很棒的地方：

  • 认识新面孔
  • 开始自发对话
  • 从结构化会议中休息一下

如果传统的网络感觉令人生畏，那么这个空间往往更加轻松和平易近人。

同伴小组指导

同行小组指导会议为来自整个 CNCF 生态系统的经验丰富的贡献者和维护者提供小组指导。

如果您满足以下条件，这些课程特别有帮助：

  • 想要职业或开源指导
  • 陷入技术或社区问题
  • 比起大群体的环境，更喜欢集中对话

过渡到第二部分

既然您知道 KubeCon + CloudNativeCon 的不同部分如何组合在一起，下一个挑战是决定如何平衡这一周。

在下一部分中，我将分享实用建议和多次参加活动的经验教训、我希望早点知道的事情以及我现在采取的不同做法。

1.在到达之前制定您的日程安排

在 KubeCon + CloudNativeCon 开始之前，您可以为自己做的最好的事情之一就是提前花一些时间制定您的日程安排。

KubeCon + CloudNativeCon Europe 官方日程已在线提供，您可以登录为您计划参加的会议添加书签。

我强烈建议在活动周之前进行此操作，而不是前一天晚上。它可以帮助您：

  • 了解您的兴趣自然流向
  • 避免超额预订
  • 更加关注您参加的活动（以及跳过的活动）

此外，下载 Sched，移动应用；它在会议期间非常有用，可以轻松访问和查找房间位置。

一个重要的实用技巧：始终为每个时间段选择至少两个会话。
房间可能很快就满了，会谈可能比预期的间隔更远，或者您可能会在最后一刻决定留下来进行对话。拥有备用选项意味着如果 A 计划不起作用，您就不会浪费时间或感到压力。

2.在打开日程表之前先决定你要去的原因

在为讲座添加书签或下载应用程序之前，暂停并回答一个问题：

我想从 KubeCon + CloudNativeCon 2026 中得到什么？

一些例子：

  • 为您的平台或内部 Kubernetes 路线图提供更清晰的方向
  • 深入研究一两个技术领域（不是十个）
  • 维护者或贡献者对话
  • 职业发展：知名度、工作机会或同行认可
  • 社区能量和动力（这一点被低估了）

如果您没有预先定义这一点，时间表将为您定义它，这通常会导致筋疲力尽，而不是洞察力。

3.有意识地安排你的日程

KubeCon + CloudNativeCon 日程安排包含适合所有技术兴趣和专业水平的选项。 2026 年，主题比以往任何时候都更广泛、更成熟：平台工程、人工智能工作负载、安全性、可观察性、成本和实际操作。

我的规则拇指：

  • 选择您非常关心的 1 个主要主题
  • 选择您感兴趣的 1 个次要主题
  • 忽略其余部分，不要感到内疚

你不需要参加所有的事情。许多谈话都会被记录下来。您稍后无法重播的是：

  • 走廊谈话
  • 演讲后问题
  • 自发的“我们在产品中尝试过这个，但失败了”的故事

这些才是真正学习发生的地方。

4.使用谈话作为对话的开始，而不是目的地

心态的转变改变了我的 KubeCon + CloudNativeCon：

会谈不是主要活动。人们是。

不要整天在房间之间奔波：

  • 会议结束后留下来与演讲者交谈
  • 询问其他与会者为什么选择相同的演讲
  • 用一次精彩的演讲引发三场有意义的对话

您会比幻灯片更长久地记住这些讨论。

5.如果您性格内向、疲倦或管理健康：没关系

KubeCon + CloudNativeCon 通常与高能量、可见性和网络联系在一起，但参与的方式有很多，并以您自己的方式使其变得有意义。

您可以：

  • 跳过会话进行休息
  • 对话次数较少但更深入
  • 对晚间活动说不
  • 在活动的安静房间和休闲区享受一些“属于自己”的时间

6.在离开之前定义您的“成功标准”

在最后一天结束之前，问问自己：

  • 我学到了可以在工作中应用的东西吗？
  • 我是否至少遇到了一个我想与之保持联系的人？
  • 我是否获得了清晰的认识，而不仅仅是信息？

如果答案是肯定的，KubeCon + CloudNativeCon 就有效。

其他一切都是奖励。

最终想法

凭借意图、界限和好奇心，KubeCon + CloudNativeCon Europe 2026 可以：

  • 接地
  • 鼓舞人心
  • 而且真正有用

我很高兴见到您，如果您要去的话，我希望您离开时保存的幻灯片更少，建立的联系更有意义。我也希望在我的闪电演讲：“没有门户？没问题！我们如何扩展我们的仅包含 Terraform 和 ArgoCD 的平台”
尚未注册 KubeCon + CloudNativeCon 还没有？还有时间！ 立即注册。

item.title: 【KubeCon + CloudNativeCon Europe 2026 Co-located Event Deep Dive: Kubernetes on Edge Day】KubeCon + CloudNativeCon Europe 2026 同期举办活动深入探讨：边缘日上的 Kubernetes
item.description:
【

Kubernetes on Edge Day returns to KubeCon + CloudNativeCon Europe 2026 with a continued focus on where cloud native technologies meet the realities of distributed, resource-constrained, and often unpredictable environments. First launched at KubeCon + CloudNativeCon Europe in 2022, Edge Day has steadily grown alongside the ecosystem, reflecting how Kubernetes is increasingly deployed beyond traditional data centers and public clouds.

In 2026, the conversation is more relevant than ever. Enterprises are navigating a paradox: legacy hardware and decentralized environments must now support modern workloads such as AI and machine learning. Edge Day creates space for practitioners to explore how Kubernetes adapts and thrives in these complex conditions.

Who will get the most out of attending this event?

Kubernetes on Edge Day welcomes anyone running Kubernetes or cloud native technologies outside of traditional data center or public cloud environments. This includes platform engineers, developers, operators, students, and business leaders who are working with distributed systems across retail stores, factory floors, vehicles, satellites, and other edge locations.

The agenda is intentionally diverse in both industry representation and perspective. Whether you are just starting to explore edge use cases or already operating production workloads at scale, the day is designed to provide insights that are practical, relevant, and grounded in real-world experience.

What is new and different this year?

This year’s program takes a deliberate approach to staying close to the core of true edge computing, while acknowledging that edge is no longer a niche topic. The reality is that edge computing is influencing nearly every vertical and technology domain.

Submissions span artificial intelligence at the edge, telco deployments, the role and impact of data on Kubernetes, and the importance of observability at scale for distributed systems. Together, these sessions tell a cohesive story about Kubernetes at the edge and how it is evolving across industries.

What will the day look like?

The event begins in the main room with a welcome from the co-chairs, followed by featured sessions that dive directly into the day’s themes. Throughout the schedule, attendees can expect dedicated breaks for coffee and networking, creating space to connect with project leaders, maintainers, and fellow practitioners.

The format blends insightful talks, open discussions, and hands-on knowledge sharing. As with all co-located events, the day serves as a focused preview of deeper conversations that continue throughout the main KubeCon + CloudNativeCon Europe program.

Should I do any homework first?

Everyone is welcome. The agenda includes a mix of beginner-friendly and advanced sessions, making the event accessible regardless of prior experience. Because sessions are limited in time, attendees are encouraged to write down their questions and engage directly with speakers to explore topics more deeply in true open source fashion.

For those newer to the space, it can be helpful to reflect on how Kubernetes is currently being used in your organization, especially in environments outside centralized infrastructure. Coming prepared with real-world questions will help you make the most of the day.

Find your community!

Kubernetes on Edge Day strengthens the community by creating a dedicated space for peers to share successes and failures in the gritty reality of running Kubernetes and cloud native technologies at the edge. These are real-world deployments that power everyday experiences for millions of people, often invisibly.

By bringing together diverse voices from around the globe, the event reinforces that Kubernetes at the edge is not theoretical. It is real Kubernetes, running in real environments, solving real problems. Edge Day provides a forum to connect with others navigating similar challenges and to build lasting relationships within this growing corner of the cloud native ecosystem.

Don’t forget to register for KubeCon + CloudNativeCon Europe 2026.

】

边缘日上的 Kubernetes 重返 KubeCon + CloudNativeCon Europe 2026，继续关注云原生技术如何满足分布式、资源受限且通常不可预测的环境的现实。 Edge Day 于 2022 年在欧洲 KubeCon + CloudNativeCon 上首次推出，并与生态系统一起稳步发展，反映出 Kubernetes 如何越来越多地部署在传统数据中心和公共云之外。

到 2026 年，对话比以往任何时候都更加有意义。企业正在面临一个悖论：传统硬件和去中心化环境现在必须支持人工智能和机器学习等现代工作负载。 Edge Day 为从业者创造了探索 Kubernetes 如何在这些复杂条件下适应和发展的空间。

谁将从参加本次活动中获益最多？

Kubernetes Edge Day 欢迎任何在传统数据中心或公共云环境之外运行 Kubernetes 或云原生技术的人。这包括在零售商店、工厂车间、车辆、卫星和其他边缘位置使用分布式系统的平台工程师、开发人员、运营商、学生和商业领袖。

议程有意在行业代表性和观点方面多样化。无论您是刚刚开始探索边缘用例，还是已经大规模运行生产工作负载，这一天都旨在提供实用、相关且基于现实经验的见解。

今年有什么新的和不同的？

今年的计划采取了一种深思熟虑的方法来接近真正的边缘计算的核心，同时承认边缘不再是一个小众话题。现实情况是，边缘计算正在影响几乎所有垂直领域和技术领域。

提交的内容涵盖边缘人工智能、电信部署、数据对 Kubernetes 的作用和影响，以及分布式系统大规模可观测性的重要性。这些会议共同讲述了一个关于边缘 Kubernetes 及其如何跨行业发展的连贯故事。

这一天会是什么样子？

活动在主房间开始，由联合主席致欢迎辞，随后是直接探讨当天主题的特色会议。在整个日程安排中，与会者可以享受专门的休息时间喝咖啡和交流，为与项目负责人、维护人员和其他从业者建立联系创造空间。

这种形式融合了深刻的演讲、公开的讨论和实践知识共享。与所有同期举办的活动一样，这一天是在 KubeCon + CloudNativeCon Europe 主要计划中持续进行的更深入对话的集中预览。

我应该先做作业吗？

欢迎大家。议程包括适合初学者的课程和高级课程，无论以前的经验如何，都可以轻松参加该活动。由于会议时间有限，我们鼓励与会者写下他们的问题并直接与演讲者互动，以真正的开源方式更深入地探讨主题。

对于该领域的新手来说，反思 Kubernetes 目前在您的组织中的使用方式可能会有所帮助，尤其是在集中式基础设施之外的环境中。准备好现实世界的问题将帮助您充分利用这一天。

找到您的社区！

边缘日上的 Kubernetes 通过为同行创建一个专门的空间来分享在边缘运行 Kubernetes 和云原生技术的严峻现实中的成功和失败，从而增强了社区的实力。这些是现实世界的部署，通常在无形中为数百万人的日常体验提供支持。

通过汇集来自全球各地的不同声音，该活动强调了边缘的 Kubernetes 并不是理论上的。它是真正的 Kubernetes，运行在真实环境中，解决真实问题。 Edge Day 提供了一个论坛，让其他人能够应对类似的挑战，并在云原生生态系统这个不断发展的角落建立持久的关系。

不要忘记注册参加 KubeCon + CloudNativeCon Europe 2026。

item.title: 【KubeCon + CloudNativeCon Europe 2026 Co-located Event Deep Dive: BackstageCon】KubeCon + CloudNativeCon Europe 2026 同期举办活动深入探讨：BackstageCon
item.description:
【

BackstageCon began in 2022 as the first dedicated conference for the Backstage ecosystem, bringing together adopters and contributors around the open source developer portal platform originally created at Spotify. As cloud native adoption continues to scale, platform engineering and internal developer portals have become critical to managing the growing complexity of Kubernetes, microservices, and distributed systems.

Right now, cloud native adoption is colliding with AI-accelerated software delivery. Teams can generate and ship code faster than they can maintain ownership, documentation, security, and operational clarity. BackstageCon matters because the community needs shared patterns for making the portal and catalog the trusted context layer that AI copilots and agents can safely use — with golden paths, standardized metadata, and governance at the core.

Who will get the most out of attending this event?

BackstageCon is especially valuable for platform engineering and internal developer platform teams building or operating Backstage in production. These teams will gain practical patterns for scaling Backstage across large organizations, from catalog structure and governance to plugin ecosystems, observability, and AI integration.

Practitioners already running Backstage will benefit from real-world case studies and operational lessons. AI-forward organizations will see how to manage models, agents, and AI workflows as first-class entities within the developer portal. Maintainers and plugin developers can better understand ecosystem direction and integration best practices. Newcomers will walk away with a clear picture of what mature, production-grade Backstage implementations look like and where the project is heading.

What is new and different this year?

This year brings a stronger focus on AI and how it intersects with platform engineering. Sessions such as Agentic Backstage: How to Manage an AI Software Catalog and Modern AI IDP: The Backstage + Kubeflow Way reflect a shift toward integrating AI workflows, models, and agents directly into the internal developer platform.

There is also expanded emphasis on developer experience and ecosystem maturity. Topics such as runtime plugins, platform CLIs, and building a healthy Backstage plugin ecosystem highlight a move beyond initial adoption toward long-term sustainability and extensibility. Observability remains a key theme, with sessions exploring how to instrument Backstage with OpenTelemetry and apply production-grade practices to the portal itself.

What will the day look like?

The day begins with welcome remarks followed by keynotes and practitioner talks exploring unified developer experience, Backstage philosophy, and the challenges of scaling platforms in an AI-enabled world.

Mid-morning sessions include lightning talks, sponsored keynotes, and deeper technical discussions on scaling Backstage and managing AI-oriented software catalogs. After lunch, the focus shifts to more advanced technical sessions covering observability, runtime plugins, case studies, and platform tooling.

Throughout the day, attendees can expect panel discussions, lightning talks, and networking breaks that encourage community interaction and knowledge sharing. The event concludes with closing remarks that tie together the day’s insights and themes.

Should I do any homework first?

While no prior experience is required, some background knowledge will help you get more value from the day. A basic understanding of Backstage — including the software catalog, plugins, and templates — is helpful. Familiarity with platform engineering concepts and internal developer platforms will also provide useful context.

Working knowledge of cloud native technologies such as Kubernetes, CI/CD, and microservices will make the technical sessions easier to follow. For AI-focused sessions, it is helpful to understand how organizations are operationalizing AI and machine learning workloads, including models, agents, and pipelines.

It can also be useful to reflect on your own organization’s developer experience challenges before attending. Many talks focus on governance, scaling, and real-world production lessons, so having your own context in mind will make it easier to apply what you learn.

Find your community!

BackstageCon strengthens the community by bringing together adopters, maintainers, and contributors in a space dedicated entirely to Backstage and platform engineering. It creates opportunities to share production stories, surface common challenges, and align on best practices.

By spotlighting plugin development, governance, and maintainer perspectives, the event supports a healthy and sustainable ecosystem. As cloud native and AI complexity grow, BackstageCon provides a forum for collaboration and shared learning, helping teams move forward together with clearer patterns and a stronger sense of direction.

Don’t forget to register for KubeCon + CloudNativeCon Europe 2026.

】

BackstageCon 于 2022 年开始，是 Backstage 生态系统的首个专门会议，汇聚了最初在 Spotify 创建的开源开发者门户平台的采用者和贡献者。随着云原生采用的不断扩大，平台工程和内部开发人员门户对于管理 Kubernetes、微服务和分布式系统日益复杂的情况变得至关重要。

目前，云原生的采用正在与人工智能加速的软件交付发生冲突。团队生成和交付代码的速度比维护所有权、文档、安全性和操作清晰度的速度要快。 BackstageCon 很重要，因为社区需要共享模式来使门户和目录成为 AI 副驾驶员和代理可以安全使用的可信上下文层 - 以黄金路径、标准化元数据和治理为核心。

谁将从参加本次活动中获益最多？

BackstageCon 对于平台工程和在生产中构建或运营 Backstage 的内部开发人员平台团队特别有价值。这些团队将获得在大型组织中扩展 Backstage 的实用模式，从目录结构和治理到插件生态系统、可观察性和人工智能集成。

已经运行 Backstage 的从业者将从现实案例研究和操作课程中受益。人工智能前沿组织将了解如何在开发人员门户中将模型、代理和人工智能工作流程作为一流实体进行管理。维护人员和插件开发人员可以更好地了解生态系统方向和集成最佳实践。新来者将清楚地了解成熟的生产级后台实现是什么样子以及项目的发展方向。

今年有什么新的和不同的？

今年人们更加关注人工智能及其与平台工程的交叉。 Agentic Backstage：如何管理 AI 软件目录和现代 AI IDP：后台 + Kubeflow 方式等会议反映了将 AI 工作流程、模型和代理直接集成到内部开发人员平台的转变。

开发者经验和生态系统成熟度也得到了更加重视。运行时插件、平台 CLI 和构建健康的 Backstage 插件生态系统等主题强调了从最初采用到长期可持续性和可扩展性的转变。可观察性仍然是一个关键主题，会议探讨了如何使用 OpenTelemetry 来检测 Backstage 并将生产级实践应用于门户本身。

这一天会是什么样子？

这一天以欢迎致辞开始，随后是主题演讲和从业者演讲，探讨统一的开发者体验、后台理念以及在人工智能支持下扩展平台的挑战编辑世界。

上午的会议包括闪电演讲、赞助主题演讲以及有关扩展 Backstage 和管理面向人工智能的软件目录的更深入的技术讨论。午餐后，重点转移到更高级的技术会议，涵盖可观察性、运行时插件、案例研究和平台工具。

全天，与会者可以期待小组讨论、闪电演讲和交流休息时间，以鼓励社区互动和知识共享。活动以总结当天的见解和主题的闭幕词结束。

我应该先做作业吗？

虽然不需要任何经验，但一些背景知识将帮助您从这一天获得更多价值。对 Backstage 的基本了解（包括软件目录、插件和模板）很有帮助。熟悉平台工程概念和内部开发人员平台也将提供有用的背景信息。

云原生技术（例如 Kubernetes、CI/CD 和微服务）的应用知识将使技术会议更容易遵循。对于以人工智能为中心的会议，了解组织如何实施人工智能和机器学习工作负载（包括模型、代理和管道）会很有帮助。

在参加之前反思一下您自己组织的开发人员体验挑战也很有用。许多讲座都集中在治理、扩展和现实世界的生产课程上，因此记住您自己的背景将使您更容易应用所学到的知识。

找到您的社区！

BackstageCon 通过将采用者、维护者和贡献者聚集在一个完全致力于 Backstage 和平台工程的空间中来加强社区。它创造了分享生产故事、面对共同挑战和协调最佳实践的机会。

通过关注插件开发、治理和维护者的观点，该活动支持健康且可持续的生态系统。随着云原生和人工智能复杂性的增长，BackstageCon 提供了一个协作和共享学习的论坛，帮助团队以更清晰的模式和更强的方向感共同前进。

不要忘记注册参加 KubeCon + CloudNativeCon Europe 2026。

item.title: 【Kubernetes WG Serving concludes following successful advancement of AI inference support】成功推进人工智能推理支持后，Kubernetes WG Serving 圆满结束
item.description:
【

The Kubernetes Working Group (WG) Serving was created to support development of the AI inference stack on Kubernetes. The goal of this working group was to ensure that Kubernetes is an orchestration platform of choice for inference workloads. This goal has been accomplished, and the working group is now being disbanded.

WG Serving formed workstreams to collect requirements from various model servers, hardware providers, and inference vendors. This work resulted in a common understanding of inference workload specifics and trends and laid the foundation for improvements across many SIGs in Kubernetes.

The working group oversaw several key evolutions related to load balancing and workloads. The inference gateway was adopted as a request scheduler. Multiple groups have worked to standardize AI gateway functionality, and early inference gateway participants went on to seed agent networking work in SIG Network.

The use cases and problem statements gathered by the working group informed the design of AIBrix.

Many of the unresolved problems in distributed inference — especially benchmarking and recommended best practices — have been picked up by the llm-d project, which hybridizes the infrastructure and ML ecosystems and is better able to steer model server co-evolution.

In particular, llm-d and AIBrix represent more appropriate forums for driving requirements to Kubernetes SIGs than this working group. llm-d’s goal is to provide well-lit paths for achieving state-of-the-art inference and aims to provide recommendations that can compose into existing inference user platforms. AIBrix provides a complete platform solution for cost-efficient LLM inference.

WG Serving helped with Kubernetes AI Conformance requirements. The llm-d project is leveraging multiple components from the profile and making recommendations to end users consistent with Kubernetes direction (including Kueue, inference gateway, LWS, DRA, and related efforts). Widely adopted patterns and solutions are expected to go into the conformance program.

All efforts currently running inside WG Serving can be migrated to other working groups or directly to SIGs. Requirements will be discussed in SIGs and in the llm-d community. Specifically:

  • Autoscaling-related questions — mostly related to fast bootstrap — will be discussed in SIG Node or SIG Scheduling.
  • Multi-host, multi-node work can continue as part of SIG Apps (for example, for the LWS project), and DRA requirements will be discussed in WG Device Management.
  • Orchestration topics will be covered by SIG Scheduling and SIG Node.
  • Requirements for DRA will be discussed in WG Device Management.

The Gateway API Inference Extension project is already sponsored by SIG Network and will remain there. The Serving Catalog work can be moved to the Inference Perf project. Originally it was designed for a larger scope, but it has been used mostly for inference performance.

The Inference Perf project is sponsored by SIG Scalability, and no change of ownership is needed.

CNCF thanks all contributors who participated in WG Serving and helped advance Kubernetes as a platform for AI inference workloads.

】

Kubernetes 服务工作组 (WG) 的成立是为了支持 Kubernetes 上的 AI 推理堆栈的开发。该工作组的目标是确保 Kubernetes 成为推理工作负载的首选编排平台。这个目标已经实现，工作组现已解散。

WG Serving 形成了工作流，以收集来自各种模型服务器、硬件提供商和推理供应商的需求。这项工作使人们对推理工作负载的具体情况和趋势达成了共识，并为 Kubernetes 中许多 SIG 的改进奠定了基础。

工作组监督了与负载平衡和工作负载相关的几项关键演变。采用推理网关作为请求调度器。多个小组致力于标准化 AI 网关功能，早期推理网关参与者继续在 SIG Network 中进行种子代理网络工作。

工作组收集的用例和问题陈述为 AIBrix 的设计提供了信息。

分布式推理中许多未解决的问题（尤其是基准测试和推荐的最佳实践）已被 llm-d 项目解决，该项目混合了基础设施和机器学习生态系统，能够更好地引导模型服务器共同进化。

特别是，llm-d 和 AIBrix 代表了比该工作组更适合推动 Kubernetes SIG 需求的论坛。 llm-d 的目标是为实现最先进的推理提供良好的途径，并旨在提供可纳入现有推理用户平台的建议。 AIBrix 为经济高效的 LLM 推理提供了完整的平台解决方案。

WG Serving 帮助满足 Kubernetes AI 一致性 要求。 llm-d 项目正在利用配置文件中的多个组件，并向最终用户提供符合 Kubernetes 方向的建议（包括 Kueue、推理网关、LWS、DRA 和相关工作）。广泛采用的模式和解决方案预计将纳入一致性计划。

当前在 WG Serving 内部运行的所有工作都可以迁移到其他工作组或直接迁移到 SIG。要求将在 SIG 和 llm-d 社区中讨论。具体来说：

  • 与自动扩缩相关的问题（主要与快速引导相关）将在 SIG 节点或 SIG 调度中讨论。
  • 多主机、多节点工作可以作为 SIG 应用的一部分继续进行（例如，LWS 项目），DRA 要求将在设备管理工作组中讨论。
  • 编排主题将由 SIG 调度和 SIG 节点涵盖。
  • DRA 的要求将在设备管理工作组中讨论。

网关API Inference Extension 项目已由 SIG Network 赞助，并将保留在那里。Serving Catalog 工作可以移至 Inference Perf 项目。最初它是为更大范围设计的，但现在主要用于推理性能。

Inference Perf 项目由 SIG Scalability 赞助，无需更改所有权。

CNCF 感谢所有参与 WG Serving 并帮助推动 Kubernetes 成为 AI 推理工作负载平台的贡献者。

item.title: 【CNCF joins 2026 Google Summer of Code as mentoring organization: Calling all contributors!】CNCF 作为指导组织加入 2026 年 Google 编程之夏：召集所有贡献者！
item.description:
【

We’re pleased to share that the Cloud Native Computing Foundation (CNCF) has once again been selected as a mentoring organization for the 2026 edition of Google Summer of Code (GSoC). Our first selection was back in 2017, and we have been supporting and mentoring cloud native technologists ever since. 

What is GSoC?

Google Summer of Code is a global program that introduces new contributors to open source communities through structured, mentored projects. Each year, selected participants work closely with experienced maintainers to design, build, and deliver meaningful technical contributions. For many, GSoC is the beginning of a long-term journey in open source.

CNCF’s participation reflects our ongoing commitment to growing sustainable communities around cloud native technologies. Open source thrives when new contributors feel welcomed, supported, and empowered to make an impact. Through GSoC, contributors have the opportunity to engage deeply with CNCF-hosted projects and collaborate directly with the maintainers who steward them.

The next important phase for prospective contributors runs through March 15, 2026. During this period, candidates are encouraged to actively engage with mentoring organizations, discuss potential project ideas, and begin drafting their proposals. This is a critical time to connect with project communities, ask thoughtful questions, and refine ideas in collaboration with mentors.

Important Dates and timeline

While the full program timeline can be found on the official GSoC website, here are a few key milestones:

  1. February 19 – March 15, 2026: Contributors discuss ideas with mentoring organizations
  2. March 16, 2026 : GSoC contributor application period opens
  3. March 31, 2026: GSoC contributor application period closes 

Be sure to check the GSoC website regularly for the most up-to-date information on deadlines and program details. We encourage all interested contributors to carefully review the official timeline and program guidelines to ensure they meet eligibility requirements and submission deadlines.

How to get started

If you’re interested in participating as a mentee:

  1. Explore project ideas: Using our Project Ideas page, identify projects that spark your interest.
  2. Connect with Mentors: Reach out using upstream issues listed in each project description (don’t reach out directly to mentors via email). Introduce yourself, discuss the idea, and ask questions.
  3. Draft Your Proposal: Once applications open, submit a clear, concise proposal outlining your approach, timeline, and goals.
  4. Stay Informed: Follow the official GSoC timeline and guidelines. Watch for announcements on the GSoC website and the CNCF communication channels.

We strongly encourage interested contributors to begin participating early. Successful proposals are typically shaped through discussion, iteration, and demonstrated engagement with the project community. 

CNCF is home to a diverse ecosystem of open source projects across areas such as platform engineering, observability, runtime, security, and cloud native infrastructure. GSoC projects span a range of technical domains, and contributors are matched with mentors who guide them through structured milestones and regular feedback cycles.

If you are a project maintainer and are considering mentoring during the GSoC 2026 cycle, please submit your ideas using this template available on GitHub.

Strengthening the future of cloud native

Open source innovation depends on sustained community investment. By participating in GSoC 2026, CNCF projects continue building mentorship pathways that welcome new voices and perspectives into the cloud native ecosystem.

If you’re ready to deepen your open source experience, collaborate with experienced maintainers, and make a meaningful impact, we encourage you to start engaging with CNCF project communities today.

We look forward to seeing your ideas.

】

我们很高兴地宣布，云原生计算基金会 (CNCF) 再次被选为 2026 年版 Google 编程之夏 (GSoC) 的指导组织。我们的第一次选择是在 2017 年，从那时起我们一直在支持和指导云原生技术人员。 

什么是 GSoC？

Google Summer of Code 是一项全球计划，旨在通过结构化的指导项目向开源社区引入新的贡献者。每年，选定的参与者都会与经验丰富的维护人员密切合作，设计、构建和交付有意义的技术贡献。对于许多人来说，GSoC 是长期开源之旅的开始。

CNCF 的参与体现了我们对围绕云原生技术发展可持续社区的持续承诺。当新的贡献者感到受到欢迎、支持并有权产生影响时，开源就会蓬勃发展。通过 GSoC，贡献者有机会深入参与 CNCF 托管的项目，并直接与管理它们的维护人员合作。

潜在贡献者的下一个重要阶段将持续到 2026 年 3 月 15 日。在此期间，鼓励候选人积极与指导组织接触，讨论潜在的项目想法，并开始起草他们的提案。这是与项目社区建立联系、提出深思熟虑的问题以及与导师合作完善想法的关键时刻。

重要日期和时间表

虽然完整的计划时间表可以在 GSoC 官方网站上找到，但以下是一些关键的里程碑：

  • 2026 年 2 月 19 日至 3 月 15 日：贡献者与指导组织讨论想法
  • 2026 年 3 月 16 日：GSoC 贡献者申请期开启
  • 2026 年 3 月 31 日：GSoC 贡献者申请期结束

    • 请务必定期查看 GSoC 网站，了解有关截止日期和计划详细信息的最新信息。我们鼓励所有感兴趣的贡献者仔细查看官方时间表和计划指南，以确保他们符合资格要求和提交截止日期。

    如何开始

    如果您有兴趣作为学员参与：

  • 探索项目创意：使用我们的项目创意页面，找出激发您兴趣的项目。
  • 与导师联系：利用每个项目描述中列出的上游问题进行联系（不要通过电子邮件直接与导师联系）。自我介绍、讨论想法并提出问题。
  • 起草您的提案：申请开放后，提交一份清晰、简洁的提案，概述您的方法、时间表和目标。
  • 随时了解情况：遵循官方 GSoC 时间表和指南。请留意 GSoC 网站和 CNCF 沟通渠道上的公告。

    • 我们强烈鼓励感兴趣的贡献者尽早开始参与。成功的提案通常是通过讨论、迭代和与项目社区的参与来形成的。 

    CNCF 是跨平台工程、可观察性、运行时、安全性和云原生基础设施等领域的多元化开源项目生态系统的所在地。 GSoC 项目跨越一系列技术领域，贡献者会与导师相匹配，指导他们完成结构化的里程碑和定期反馈周期。

    如果您是项目维护者并考虑在 GSoC 2026 周期期间进行指导，请使用此模板提交您的想法GitHub 上提供

    加强云原生的未来

    开源创新取决于持续的社区投资。通过参与 GSoC 2026，CNCF 项目继续构建指导途径，欢迎新的声音和观点进入云原生生态系统。

    如果您准备好加深您的开源体验、与经验丰富的维护人员合作并产生有意义的影响，我们鼓励您立即开始加入 CNCF 项目社区。

    我们期待看到您的想法。

item.title: 【Exposing Spin apps on SpinKube with GatewayAPI】使用 GatewayAPI 在 SpinKube 上公开 Spin 应用程序
item.description:
【

The Gateway API isn’t just an “Ingress v2”, it’s an entirely revamped approach for exposing services from within Kubernetes and eliminates the need of encoding routing capabilities into vendor-specific, unstructured annotations. In this post, we will explore how to expose WebAssembly applications built using the CNCF Spin framework and served by SpinKube using the Gateway API.

What is SpinKube

SpinKube, a CNCF sandbox project, is an open-source stack for running serverless WebAssembly applications (Spin apps) on top of Kubernetes. Although SpinKube leverages Kubernetes primitives like Deployments, Services and Pods, there are no containers involved for running your serverless Spin apps at all. Instead, it leverages a containerd-shim implementation and spawns processes on the underlying Kubernetes worker nodes for running Spin apps.

You can learn more about SpinKube and find detailed instructions on how to deploy SpinKube to your Kubernetes cluster at https://spinkube.dev.

What is Gateway API

The Gateway API is the modern, role-oriented successor to the legacy Ingress resource, designed to provide a more expressive and extensible networking interface for Kubernetes. Unlike Ingress, which often relies on a messy sprawl of vendor-specific annotations to handle complex logic, the Gateway API breaks traffic management into atomic resources —GatewayClass, Gateway, and routes (like HTTPRoute or GRPCRoute).

This separation allows infrastructure admins to manage the entry points while giving developers control over how their specific services are exposed, enabling native support for advanced traffic patterns like canary rollouts, header-based routing, and traffic mirroring without the need for bespoke configurations.

To dive deeper into the technical specifications and resource hierarchy, head over to the official Gateway API documentation.

Provisioning a Kubernetes cluster, installing SpinKube and implementing Spin apps are considered beyond the scope of this article. However, you can head over to https://github.com/akamai-developers/exposing-spin-apps-with-gatway-api – a repository containing all source code, along with the necessary instructions for setting up a LKE cluster with SpinKube.

To follow the article’s demo, you’ll deploy the required artifacts to your Kubernetes cluster. Make sure you have the following tools installed:

  • The spin CLI (see https://spinframework.dev for installation instructions)
  • Rust along with the wasm32-wasip1 target (See https://rust-lang.github.io/rustup/ for detailed instructions)
  • Both CLIs kubectl and helm for interacting with your Kubernetes cluster(See https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/tools/ and https://helm.sh/docs/intro/install respectively)
    • Once you’ve installed kubectl download necessary credentials for accessing your Kubernetes cluster

Build and deploy the Spin apps to Kubernetes

Let’s start by compiling the source code of our sample Spin apps down to WebAssembly. Doing so is as easy as executing the spin build command from within each application folder:

# Build the greeter application
pushd apps/greeter
spin build

 Building component greeter with `cargo build --target wasm32-wasip1 --release`
     Finished `release` profile [optimized] target(s) in 0.21s
 Finished building all Spin components

popd

# Build the prime_numbers application
pushd apps/prime-numbers
spin build

  Building component prime-numbers with `cargo build --target wasm32-wasip1 --release`
    Finished `release` profile [optimized] target(s) in 0.18s
  Finished building all Spin components
popd

Once the application has been compiled, we use the spin registry push to distribute it as OCI artifact. (If your OCI compliant registry requires authentication, you must login first. Use the spin registry login to authenticate before trying to push).

Tip: For testing purposes, we’ll use ttl.sh an anonymous and ephemeral OCI compliant registry, which allows us to store our applications for 24 hours by simply specifying the TTL as a tag.

# specify variables
greeter_app_artifact=ttl.sh/spin-greeter:24h
primenumbers_app_artifact=ttl.sh/spin-prime-numbers:24h

# optional: Authenticate against registry
oci_reg_server=
oci_reg_user=
oci_reg_password=
spin registry login $oci_reg_server -u $oci_reg_user -p $oci_reg_password

# distribute the Spin applications
pushd apps/greeter
spin registry push $greeter_app_artifact --build
popd

pushd apps/prime-numbers
spin registry push $primenumbers_app_artifact --build
popd

Finally, we use the spin kube scaffold command for generating the necessary Kubernetes manifests.

Tip: Spin does not have any opinions on how you deploy resources to your Kubernetes cluster. You can either use kubectl, create a Helm chart and deploy it using the helm CLI, or describe the desired state and deploy it with GitOps.

For the sake of this article, we’ll simply pipe the generated manifest to kubectl apply. The actual manifests are shown here for illustration purposes:

# Deploy the Spin applications to Kubernets
spin kube scaffold --from $greeter_app_artifact | kubectl apply -f -
spin kube scaffold --from $primenumbers_app_artifact | kubectl apply -f -

apiVersion: core.spinkube.dev/v1alpha1
kind: SpinApp
metadata:
  name: spin-greeter
spec:
  image: "ttl.sh/spin-greeter:24h"
  executor: containerd-shim-spin
  replicas: 2
---
apiVersion: core.spinkube.dev/v1alpha1
kind: SpinApp
metadata:
  name: spin-prime-numbers
spec:
  image: "ttl.sh/spin-prime-numbers:24h"
  executor: containerd-shim-spin
  replicas: 2

Obviously, there are additional knobs you can turn when executing spin kube scaffold, I highly encourage you to checkout the documentation for the command by providing the --help flag.

Testing the Spin app

We use traditional port-forwarding provided by kubectl to verify that both Spin applications runs as expected:

kubectl port-forward svc/spin-greeter 8080:80

Sent a GET request to the application using curl:

curl -i localhost:8080/hello/Akamai%20Developers

HTTP/1.1 200 OK
content-type: text/plain
transfer-encoding: chunked
date: Mon, 19 Jan 2026 13:55:34 GMT

Hello, Akamai Developers!

Next, let’s test the second Spin application:

kubectl port-forward svc/spin-prime-numbers 8080:80



Again, use curl to invoke one of the endpoints exposed by the Spin app:

curl -i localhost:8080/above/42

HTTP/1.1 200 OK
transfer-encoding: chunked
date: Mon, 19 Jan 2026 17:05:02 GMT

Next prime number above 42 is 43


Now that both apps are working, you can terminate port-forwarding again (`CTRL+C) and dive into exposing both Spin apps.

Installing Gateway API CRDs and  Controller

To use the Gateway API, we must install the corresponding Gateway API resources (CRDs) on our cluster along with a Gateway API Controller.

There are several controllers available that implement the Gateway API. You can find a list of available Gateway API controllers at https://gateway-api.sigs.k8s.io/implementations/. We’ll use NGINX Gateway Fabric for now.

To install Gateway API resources run:

kubectl kustomize "https://github.com/nginx/nginx-gateway-fabric/config/crd/gateway-api/standard?ref=v2.3.0" | kubectl apply -f -

To install NGINX Gateway Fabric run:

helm install ngf oci://ghcr.io/nginx/charts/nginx-gateway-fabric --create-namespace -n nginx-gateway


Creating cluster-specific Gateway API resources

With the Gateway API controller installed, we will first deploy a Gateway to our cluster. Think of the Gateway as an entry point into your Kubernetes cluster, which could be shared across multiple applications. We’ll now create the spinkube Gateway, which will front our two Spin applications that are already running in the default namespace.

apiVersion: gateway.networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Gateway
metadata:
name: spinkube
namespace: default
spec:
gatewayClassName: nginx
listeners:
- protocol: HTTP
  port: 8080
  name: http
  allowedRoutes:
  namespaces:
  from: Same

Once you’ve deployed the Gateway, you should find a new service being provisioned to the default namespace called spinkube-nginx of type LoadBalancer once the cloud controller has acquired a public IP address, you should find it as part of the output as well.

kubectl get services

NAME            TYPE         EXTERNAL-IP
spinkube-nginx  LoadBalancer 172.238.61.25


Note down the external IP address of the spinkube-nginx service, we’ll use it in a few minutes to send requests to our Spin applications from outside of the cluster!

Creating application-specific Gateway API Resources

As we have deployed two different Spin applications to our Kubernetes cluster, we’ll also create two instances of HTTPRoute and link them to the Gateway we created in the previous section.

Tip: As managing external DNS is beyond the scope of this article, we’ll use simple PathPrefix based routing in combination with a Rewrite filter to route inbound requests to the desired Spin applications.

Create the following HTTPRoute resources in the default namespace:

apiVersion: gateway.networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: HTTPRoute
metadata:
  name: greeter
  namespace: default
spec:
  parentRefs:
  - name: spinkube
  rules:
  - backendRefs:
    - name: spin-greeter
      port: 80
    filters:
    - type: URLRewrite
      urlRewrite:
        path:
          replacePrefixMatch: /
          type: ReplacePrefixMatch
    matches:
    - path:
        type: PathPrefix
        value: /greeter
---
apiVersion: gateway.networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: HTTPRoute
metadata:
  name: prime-numbers
  namespace: default
spec:
  parentRefs:
  - name: spinkube
  rules:
  - backendRefs:
    - name: spin-prime-numbers
      port: 80
    filters:
    - type: URLRewrite
      urlRewrite:
        path:
          replacePrefixMatch: /
          type: ReplacePrefixMatch
    matches:
    - path:
        type: PathPrefix
        value: /prime-numbers

Accessing the Spin apps

Having all Kubernetes resources in place, it’s time for a final test. We discovered the public IP address associated with our Gateway earlier in this post. Let’s use curl again for sending requests to both Spin application:

# Send request to the greeter app
curl -i http:///<your_gateway_ip>:8080/greet/hello/Akamai%20Developers

HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Server: nginx
Date: Mon, 19 Jan 2026 16:37:22 GMT
Content-Type: text/plain
Transfer-Encoding: chunked
Connection: keep-alive

Hello, Akamai Developers!


# Send request to the prime-numbers app
curl -i http://<your_gateway_ip>:8080/prime-numbers/above/999

HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Server: nginx
Date: Mon, 19 Jan 2026 16:37:50 GMT
Transfer-Encoding: chunked
Connection: keep-alive

Next prime number above 999 is 1009


As you can see, our requests get routed to the desired Spin application because of the path prefix (either greeter or prime-numbers).

Conclusion

The Kubernetes Gateway API streamlines how we expose services from within a Kubernetes cluster and allows precise separation of concerns. Cloud infrastructure and cluster operators create and manage resources that could be shared across multiple applications like the Gateway, while application developers provide application (or service) specific resources such as an HTTPRoute.

Especially when running tens or hundreds of different serverless applications on top of SpinKube it’s crucial to have robust and reliable routing in place to ensure applications are accessible from outside of the cluster. The Gateway API for Kubernetes makes managing these a breeze.

Contributors from Akamai collaborate on SpinKube development to deliver this runtime across its global cloud and edge. Additional information is available.at akamai.com.







】

网关 API 不仅仅是一个“Ingress v2”，它是一种完全改进的方法，用于从 Kubernetes 内公开服务，并且无需将路由功能编码为特定于供应商的非结构化注释。在这篇文章中，我们将探讨如何公开使用 CNCF Spin 框架构建并由 SpinKube 使用 Gateway API 提供服务的 WebAssembly 应用程序。

什么是 SpinKube

SpinKube 是一个 CNCF 沙箱项目，是一个开源堆栈，用于在 Kubernetes 之上运行无服务器 WebAssembly 应用程序（Spin 应用程序）。尽管 SpinKube 利用 Kubernetes 原语（如部署、服务和 Pod），但根本不涉及运行无服务器 Spin 应用程序的容器。相反，它利用 containerd-shim 实现，并在底层 Kubernetes 工作节点上生成进程来运行 Spin 应用程序。

您可以在 https://spinkube.dev 上了解有关 SpinKube 的更多信息，并找到有关如何将 SpinKube 部署到 Kubernetes 集群的详细说明。

什么是网关 API

Gateway API 是传统 Ingress 资源的现代、面向角色的继承者，旨在为 Kubernetes 提供更具表现力和可扩展的网络接口。与 Ingress 不同，Ingress 通常依赖于混乱的供应商特定注释来处理复杂的逻辑，而 Gateway API 将流量管理分解为原子资源 - GatewayClass、Gateway 和路由（例如 HTTPRoute 或 GRPCRoute）。

这种分离允许基础设施管理员管理入口点，同时让开发人员控制其特定服务的公开方式，从而为金丝雀部署、基于标头的路由和流量镜像等高级流量模式提供本机支持，而无需定制配置。

要深入了解技术规范和资源层次结构，请访问官方 Gateway API 文档。

配置 Kubernetes 集群、安装 SpinKube 和实施 Spin 应用程序不属于本文的讨论范围。但是，您可以前往 https://github.com/akamai-developers/exusing-spin-apps-with-gatway-api – 一个包含所有源代码的存储库，以及使用 SpinKube 设置 LKE 集群的必要说明。

为了按照本文的演示进行操作，您需要将所需的工件部署到 Kubernetes 集群。确保您安装了以下工具：

  • spin CLI（请参阅https://spinframework.dev 安装说明）
  • Rust 与 wasm32-wasip1 目标一起使用（有关详细说明，请参阅 https://rust-lang.github.io/rustup/）
  • 用于与 Kubernetes 集群交互的 CLI kubectl 和 helm（分别参见 https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/tools/ 和 https://helm.sh/docs/intro/install）
    • 安装 kubectl 后，下载访问 Kubernetes 集群所需的凭据

构建 Spin 应用并将其部署到 Kubernetes

我们首先将示例 Spin 应用程序的源代码编译为 WebAssembly。这样做就像在每个应用程序文件夹中执行 spin build 命令一样简单：

# 构建greeter应用程序
推送应用程序/问候语
旋转构建

 使用“cargo build --target wasm32-wasip1 --release”构建组件欢迎程序
     在 0.21 秒内完成“release”配置文件[优化]目标
 已完成所有 Spin 组件的构建

波普德

# 构建 prime_numbers 应用程序
Pushd 应用程序/质数
旋转构建

  使用“cargo build --target wasm32-wasip1 --release”构建组件素数
    在 0.18 秒内完成“release”配置文件[优化]目标
  已完成所有 Spin 组件的构建
波普德

应用程序编译完成后，我们使用 spinregistry push 将其作为 OCI 工件进行分发。 （如果您的 OCI 兼容注册表需要身份验证，则必须先登录。在尝试推送之前使用 spin 注册表登录 进行身份验证）。

提示：出于测试目的，我们将使用 ttl.sh 一个匿名且临时的 OCI 兼容注册表，它允许我们通过简单地将 TTL 指定为标签来将应用程序存储 24 小时。

#指定变量
greeter_app_artifact=ttl.sh/spin-greeter:24h
primenumbers_app_artifact=ttl.sh/spin-prime-numbers:24h

# 可选：根据注册表进行身份验证
oci_reg_服务器=
oci_reg_user=
oci_reg_密码=
旋转注册表登录 $oci_reg_server -u $oci_reg_user -p $oci_reg_password

# 分发 Spin 应用程序
推送应用程序/问候语
spin 注册表推送 $greeter_app_artifact --build
波普德

Pushd 应用程序/质数
spin 注册表推送 $primenumbers_app_artifact --build
波普德

最后，我们使用 spin kubescaffold 命令生成必要的 Kubernetes 清单。

提示：Spin 对于如何将资源部署到 Kubernetes 集群没有任何意见。您可以使用 kubectl，创建 Helm 图表并使用 helm CLI 进行部署，或者描述所需的状态并使用 GitO 进行部署附：

在本文中，我们将简单地将生成的清单通过管道传递给 kubectl apply。出于说明目的，此处显示了实际清单：

# 将 Spin 应用程序部署到 Kubernetes
spin kube 脚手架 --来自 $greeter_app_artifact | kubectl 应用-f-
spin kube 脚手架 --来自 $primenumbers_app_artifact | kubectl 应用-f-

api版本：core.spinkube.dev/v1alpha1
种类：SpinApp
元数据：
  名称：旋转迎宾员
规格：
  图片：“ttl.sh/spin-greeter:24h”
  执行者：containerd-shim-spin
  副本：2
---
api版本：core.spinkube.dev/v1alpha1
种类：SpinApp
元数据：
  名称：自旋素数
规格：
  图片：“ttl.sh/spin-prime-numbers:24h”
  执行者：containerd-shim-spin
  副本：2

显然，在执行spin kubescaffold时，您可以转动其他旋钮，我强烈建议您通过提供--help标志来查看该命令的文档。

测试 Spin 应用

我们使用 kubectl 提供的传统端口转发来验证两个 Spin 应用程序是否按预期运行：

kubectl 端口转发 svc/spin-greeter 8080:80

使用curl:向应用程序发送 GET 请求

curl -i localhost:8080/hello/Akamai%20Developers

HTTP/1.1 200 好
内容类型：文本/纯文本
传输编码：分块
日期：2026 年 1 月 19 日星期一 13:55:34 GMT

您好，Akamai 开发人员！

接下来，让我们测试第二个 Spin 应用程序：

kubectl 端口转发 svc/spin-prime-numbers 8080:80



再次使用curl 调用Spin 应用程序公开的端点之一：

curl -i localhost:8080/above/42

HTTP/1.1 200 好
传输编码：分块
日期：2026 年 1 月 19 日星期一 17:05:02 GMT

42 以上的下一个质数是 43


现在两个应用程序都在工作，您可以再次终止端口转发 (`CTRL+C) 并深入公开两个 Spin 应用程序。

安装网关 API CRD 和控制器

要使用网关 API，我们必须在集群上安装相应的网关 API 资源 (CRD) 以及网关 API 控制器。

有多个控制器可以实现网关 API。您可以在 https://gateway-api.sigs.k8s.io/implementations/ 中找到可用网关 API 控制器的列表。我们现在将使用 NGINX Gateway Fabric。

要安装网关 API 资源，请运行：

kubectl kustomize "https://github.com/nginx/nginx-gateway-fabric/config/crd/gateway-api/standard?ref=v2.3.0" | kubectl apply -f -

要安装 NGINX Gateway Fabric，请运行：

helm install ngf oci://ghcr.io/nginx/charts/nginx-gateway-fabric --create-namespace -n nginx-gateway


创建集群特定的网关 API 资源

安装网关 API 控制器后，我们首先将网关部署到我们的集群。将网关视为 Kubernetes 集群的入口点，可以在多个应用程序之间共享。我们现在将创建 spinkube Gateway，它将位于我们已经在默认命名空间中运行的两个 Spin 应用程序的前面。

api版本：gateway.networking.k8s.io/v1
种类: 网关
元数据：
名称：斯平库贝
命名空间：默认
规格：
gatewayClassName: nginx
听众：
- 协议：HTTP
  端口：8080
  名称：http
  允许的路线：
  命名空间：
  来自：相同

部署网关后，您应该会发现一个新服务被配置到名为 spinkube-nginx 的默认命名空间，类型为 LoadBalancer。一旦云控制器获取了公共 IP 地址，您也应该会在输出中找到它。

kubectl 获取服务

名称 类型 外部 IP
spinkube-nginx负载均衡器172.238.61.25


记下spinkube-nginx服务的外部IP地址，我们将在几分钟内使用它从集群外部向我们的Spin应用程序发送请求！

创建特定于应用程序的网关 API 资源

由于我们已将两个不同的 Spin 应用程序部署到 Kubernetes 集群，因此我们还将创建两个 HTTPRoute 实例，并将它们链接到我们在上一节中创建的 Gateway。

提示：由于管理外部 DNS 超出了本文的范围，因此我们将使用基于 PathPrefix 的简单路由与 Rewrite 过滤器相结合，将入站请求路由到所需的 Spin 应用程序。

在默认命名空间中创建以下 HTTPRoute 资源：

api版本：gateway.networking.k8s.io/v1
种类：HTTP 路由
元数据：
  姓名：迎宾员
  命名空间：默认
规格：
  家长参考：
  - 名称：斯平库贝
  规则：
  - 后端参考：
    - 名称：旋转迎宾员
      端口：80
    过滤器：
    - 类型：URLRewrite
      网址重写：
        路径：
          替换前缀匹配：/
          类型：ReplacePrefixMatch
    匹配：
    - 路径：
        类型：路径前缀
        值：/问候语
---
api版本：gateway.networking.k8s.io/v1
种类：HTTP 路由
元数据：
  名称：素数
  命名空间：默认
规格：
  家长参考：
  - 名称：斯平库贝
  规则：
  - 后端参考：
    - 姓名：自旋素数
      端口：80
    过滤器：
    - 类型：URLRewrite
      网址重写：
        路径：
          替换前缀匹配：/
          类型：ReplacePrefixMatch
    匹配：
    - 路径：
        类型：路径前缀
        值：/素数

访问 Spin 应用

所有 Kubernetes 资源就位后，就可以进行最终测试了。我们在本文前面发现了与我们的网关关联的公共IP地址。让我们再次使用 curl 向两个 Spin 应用程序发送请求：

# 向greeter应用发送请求
curl -i http:///:8080/greet/hello/Akamai%20Developers

HTTP/1.1 200 好
服务器：nginx
日期：2026 年 1 月 19 日星期一 16:37:22 GMT
内容类型：文本/纯文本
传输编码：分块
连接：保持活动状态

您好，Akamai 开发人员！


# 向素数应用程序发送请求
卷曲 -i http://:8080/prime-numbers/above/999

HTTP/1.1 200 好
服务器：nginx
日期：2026 年 1 月 19 日星期一 16:37:50 GMT
传输编码：分块
连接：保持活动状态

999 以上的下一个素数是 1009


如您所见，由于路径前缀（greeter 或 prime-numbers），我们的请求被路由到所需的 Spin 应用程序。

结论

Kubernetes Gateway API 简化了我们在 Kubernetes 集群内公开服务的方式，并允许精确分离关注点。云基础设施和集群运营商创建和管理可在多个应用程序（例如网关）之间共享的资源，而应用程序开发人员则提供应用程序（或服务）特定的资源，例如 HTTPRoute。

特别是当在 SpinKube 上运行数十或数百个不同的无服务器应用程序时，拥有强大且可靠的路由以确保可以从集群外部访问应用程序至关重要。 Kubernetes 的网关 API 使管理这些变得轻而易举。

Akamai 的贡献者合作开发 SpinKube，以跨其全球云和边缘交付该运行时。如需了解更多信息，请访问 akamai.com。

item.title: 【Announcing H2 2026 KCDs】宣布 2026 年下半年 KCD
item.description:
【

We’re excited to announce the full list of Kubernetes Community Days (KCDs) for 2026! These community-organized events bring together local practitioners, adopters, and contributors to connect and share cloud native knowledge.

Insights into the Selection Process

We received an overwhelming number of exceptional KCD applications for the second half of 2026! With particularly high interest across South Asia and Europe, narrowing down the final list was a significant challenge.

To ensure a fair and impactful selection, the committee took into consideration additional strategic benchmarks beyond the standard requirements. This round, we prioritized:

  • Cross-Regional Impact: Favoring events that unite multiple communities beyond their immediate local area.
  • Regional Balance: Ensuring a balanced distribution of KCDs globally between H1 and H2.
  • Event Equity: Targeting regions that currently lack other officially supported CNCF events.
  • Sustained Maturity: Focusing on chapters with over one year of proven, consistent growth.

*Regional reference refers to Africa, Asia Pacific, Europe, Latin America, Oceania, and North America.

What’s New in 2026

This year, CNCF has introduced three tiers of KCDs to support communities of all sizes:

  • First-time KCDs – up to 200 attendees, guaranteed CNCF funding, and dedicated program support
  • Tier 1 – returning events with 350 or more attendees, eligible for funding and monthly global organizer syncs
  • Tier 2 – established events with up to 600 registrations, CNCF representation, and elevated branding

📅 H2 2026 KCDs

📅 H1 2026 KCDs

Save the dates and join us at a KCD near you!

】

我们很高兴宣布 2026 年 Kubernetes 社区日 (KCD) 的完整列表！这些社区组织的活动将本地从业者、采用者和贡献者聚集在一起，相互联系并分享云原生知识。

深入了解选择过程

我们收到了大量 2026 年下半年的优秀 KCD 申请！由于南亚和欧洲的兴趣特别浓厚，缩小最终名单的范围是一项重大挑战。

为了确保公平且有影响力的选择，委员会考虑了标准要求之外的其他战略基准。这一轮，我们优先考虑的是：

  • 跨区域影响：有利于将多个社区团结起来的活动。
  • 区域平衡：确保上半年和下半年 KCD 在全球范围内均衡分配。
  • 活动公平性：针对目前缺乏其他官方支持的 CNCF 活动的区域。
  • 持续成熟度：重点关注经过一年多时间证明持续增长的章节。

*区域指的是非洲、亚太地区、欧洲、拉丁美洲、大洋洲和北美洲。

2026 年新动态

今年，CNCF 推出了三层 KCD 来支持各种规模的社区：

  • 首次 KCD – 最多 200 名与会者，有保证的 CNCF 资金和专门的计划支持
  • 第 1 级 – 返回有 350 名或更多参加者的活动，有资格获得资助和每月全球组织者同步
  • 第 2 级 – 注册人数高达 600 人、CNCF 代表和提升品牌知名度的知名活动

📅 2026 年下半年 KCD

📅 2026 年上半年 KCD

保存日期并在您附近的 KCD 加入我们！

item.title: 【Making Harbor production-ready: Essential considerations for deployment】让 Harbour 做好生产准备：部署的基本考虑因素
item.description:
【

Harbor is an open-source container registry that secures artifacts with policies and role-based access control, ensuring images are scanned for vulnerabilities and signed as trusted. To learn more about Harbor and how to deploy it on a Virtual Machine (VM) and in Kubernetes (K8s), refer to parts 1 and 2 of the series.

While deploying Harbor is straightforward, making it production-ready requires careful consideration of several key aspects. This blog outlines critical factors to ensure your Harbor instance is robust, secure, and scalable for production environments.

For this blog, we will focus on Harbor deployed on Kubernetes via Helm as our base and provide suggestions for this specific deployment.

1. High Availability (HA) and scalability

For a production environment, single points of failure are unacceptable, especially for an image registry that will act as a central repository for storing and pulling images and artifacts for development and production applications. Thus, implementing high availability for Harbor is crucial and involves several key considerations:

  • Deploy with an Ingress: Configure a Kubernetes Service of type Ingress controller (e.g. Traefik) in front of your Harbor instances to distribute incoming traffic efficiently and provide a unified entry point along with cert-manager for certificate management. You can specify this in your values.yaml file under:
expose:
  type: ingress
  tls:
    enabled: true
    certSource: secret 
  ingress:
    hosts:
      core: harbor.yourdomain.com
    annotations:
      # Specify your ingress class
      kubernetes.io/ingress.class: traefik
      # Reference your ClusterIssuer (e.g., self-signed or internal CA)
      cert-manager.io/cluster-issuer: "harbor-cluster-issuer"

To locate your values.yaml file, refer to the previous blog.

  • Utilize multiple Harbor instances: Increase the replica count for critical Harbor components (e.g., core, jobservice, portal, registry, trivy) in your values.yaml to ensure redundancy.
core:
  replicas: 3
jobservice:
  replicas: 3
portal:
  replicas: 3
registry:
  replicas: 3
trivy:
  replicas: 3

# While not strictly for the HA of the registry itself, consider increasing exporter replicas for robust monitoring availability
exporter:
  replicas: 3

# Optionally, if using Ingress, consider increasing the Nginx replicas for improving Ingress availability
nginx:
  replicas: 3

Configure shared storage: For persistent data, configure Kubernetes StorageClasses and PersistentVolumes to use shared storage solutions like vSAN or a distributed file system. Specify these in your values.yaml under:

persistence:
  enabled: true
  resourcePolicy: "keep"
  persistentVolumeClaim:
    registry:
      #If left empty, the kubernetes cluster default storage class will be used
      storageClass: "your-storage-class"
     jobservice:
       storageClass: "your-storage-class"
     database:
       storageClass: "your-storage-class"
    redis:
      storageClass: "your-storage-class"
    trivy:
      storageClass: "your-storage-class"
  • Enable database HA (PostgreSQL): While Harbor comes with a built-in PostgreSQL database, it is not recommended for production use as it: 
  1. Lack of high availability (HA): The default internal PostgreSQL setup within the Harbor Helm chart is typically a single instance. This creates a single point of failure. If that database pod goes down, your entire Harbor instance will be unavailable.
  1. Limited scalability: An embedded database is not designed for independent scaling. If your Harbor usage grows, you might hit database performance bottlenecks that are difficult to address without disrupting Harbor itself.
  1. Complex lifecycle management: Managing backups, point-in-time recovery, patching, and upgrades for a stateful database directly within an application’s Helm chart can be significantly more complex and error-prone than with dedicated database solutions.

Thus, it is recommended to deploy a highly available PostgreSQL cluster within Kubernetes (e.g., using a Helm chart for Patroni or CloudNativePG) or leverage a managed database service outside the cluster. Configure Harbor to connect to this HA database by updating the values.yaml:

database:
  type: "external"
  external:
    host: "192.168.0.1"
    port: "5432"
    username: "user"
    password: "password"
    coreDatabase: "registry"
    # If using an existing secret, the key must be "password"
    existingSecret: ""
    # "disable" - No SSL
    # "require" - Always SSL (skip verification)
    # "verify-ca" - Always SSL (verify that the certificate presented by the
    # server was signed by a trusted CA)
    # "verify-full" - Always SSL (verify that the certification presented by the
    # server was signed by a trusted CA and the server host name matches the one
    # in the certificate)
    sslmode: "verify-full"

Implement Redis HA: Deploy a highly available Redis cluster in Kubernetes (e.g., using a Helm chart for Redis Sentinel or Redis Cluster) or utilize a managed Redis service. Configure Harbor to connect to this HA Redis instance by updating redis.type and connection details in values.yaml.

redis:
  type: external
  external:
    addr: "192.168.0.2:6397"
    sentinelMasterSet: ""
    tlsOptions:
      enable: true
    username: ""
    password: ""

2. Security best practices

Security is paramount for any production system, especially a container registry.

Enable TLS/SSL: Always enable TLS/SSL for all Harbor components.

expose:
  tls:
    enabled: true
    certSource: auto # change to manual if using cert-manager
    auto:
      commonName: ""
internalTLS:
  enabled: true
  strong_ssl_ciphers: true
  certSource: "auto"
  core:
    secretName: ""
  jobService:
    secretName: ""
  registry:
    secretName: ""
  portal:
    secretName: ""
  trivy:
    secretName: ""


Configure authentication and authorization: Leverage Harbor’s supported Authentication and Authorization mechanisms for managing access to Harbor resources. After Harbor deployment, integrate Harbor with enterprise identity providers like LDAP or OIDC by following the Harbor configuration guides: Configure LDAP/Active Directory Authentication or Configure OIDC Provider Authentication.

Implement vulnerability scanning: Ensure vulnerability scanning is enabled in values.yaml. Harbor uses Trivy by default. Verify its activation and configuration within the Helm chart.

trivy:
 enabled: true

Activate content trust: Harbor supports multiple content trust mechanisms to ensure the integrity of your artifacts. For modern OCI artifact signing, Cosign and Notation are recommended. Enforce deployment security at the project level within the Harbor UI or via the Harbor API to allow only verified images to be deployed. This ensures that only trusted and cryptographically signed images can be deployed.

  • Maintain regular updates: Regularly update your Harbor Helm chart and underlying Kubernetes components to benefit from the latest security patches and bug fixes. Use helm upgrade for this purpose.
  • Use robot accounts for automation: Use robot accounts (service accounts) in automation such as CI/CD pipelines to avoid using user credentials. This ensures the robot account with the least required privileges is used to perform the specific task it has been created for, ensuring limited scope.
  • Fine grained audit log: In Harbor v2.13.0, Harbor supports the re-direction of specific events in the audit log.  For example, an “authentication failure”  event can be configured in the audit log and forwarded to a 3rd party syslog endpoint.

3. Storage considerations

Efficient and reliable storage is critical for Harbor’s performance and stability.

  • Choose appropriate storage type: Define Kubernetes StorageClasses that align with your underlying infrastructure (e.g., nfs-client, aws-ebs, azure-disk, gcp-pd). Specify these settings in your values.yaml: 
persistence:
  enabled: true
  resourcePolicy: "keep"
  imageChartStorage:
    #Specify storage type: "filesystem", "azure", "gcs", "s3", "swift", "oss"
    type: ""
    #Configure specific storage type section based on the selected option
  • Estimate storage sizing: Carefully calculate your storage needs based on the anticipated number and size of container images, as well as your defined retention policies. Configure the size for your PersistentVolumeClaims in values.yaml.
  • Implement robust backup and recovery: Establish a comprehensive backup strategy for all Harbor data. For Kubernetes-native backups, consider using tools like Velero to back up PersistentVolumes and Kubernetes resources. For object storage, leverage the cloud provider’s backup mechanisms or external backup solutions. Regularly test your recovery procedures.
  • Configure and run garbage collection: Set up and routinely execute Harbor’s garbage collection. This can be configured through the Harbor UI by defining a schedule for automated runs to remove unused blobs and efficiently reclaim storage space.

4. Monitoring and alerting

Proactive monitoring and alerting are essential for identifying and addressing issues before they impact users.
Collect Comprehensive Metrics: Deploy Prometheus and configure it to scrape metrics from Harbor components. The Harbor Helm chart exposes Prometheus-compatible endpoints in the values.yaml file. Visualize these metrics using Grafana.

metrics:
  enabled: true
  core:
    path: /metrics
    port: 8001
  registry:
    path: /metrics
    port: 8001
  jobservice:
    path: /metrics
    port: 8001
  exporter:
    path: /metrics
    port: 8001
  serviceMonitor:
    enabled: true
    # This label ensures the prometheus operator picks up these monitors
    additionalLabels:
      release: kube-prometheus-stack

# Example Service Monitor objects:

# Harbor Core (API and Auth Performance)
apiVersion: monitoring.coreos.com/v1
kind: ServiceMonitor
metadata:
  name: harbor-core
  labels:
    app: harbor
    release: kube-prometheus-stack
spec:
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: harbor
      component: core
  endpoints:
  - port: metrics # Defaults to 8001
    path: /metrics
    interval: 30s

# Harbor Exporter (Business Metrics)
apiVersion: monitoring.coreos.com/v1
kind: ServiceMonitor
metadata:
  name: harbor-exporter
  labels:
    app: harbor
    release: kube-prometheus-stack
spec:
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: harbor
      component: exporter
  endpoints:
  - port: metrics
    path: /metrics
    interval: 60s # Scraped less frequently as these are high-level stats
  • Centralized logging: Implement a centralized logging solution within Kubernetes, such as the ELK stack (Elasticsearch, Logstash, Kibana) or Grafana with Fluentd/Fluent Bit. 
  • Configure critical alerts: Set up alerting rules in Prometheus (Alertmanager) or Grafana for critical events, such as component failures, high resource utilization (CPU/memory limits), storage nearing capacity, failed vulnerability scans, or unauthorized access attempts. Define these thresholds based on your production requirements.

5. Network configuration

Proper network configuration ensures smooth communication between Harbor components and external clients.

  • Configure ingress or load balancer and DNS resolution: As already mentioned, deploy a Kubernetes Ingress controller or Load Balancer to expose Harbor externally. Ensure proper DNS records are configured to point to your Load Balancer’s IP address.
  • Set Up proxy settings (if applicable): If Harbor components need to access external resources through a corporate proxy, configure proxy settings within values.yaml. It’s crucial to note that the proxy.components field explicitly defines which Harbor components (e.g., core, jobservice, trivy) will utilize these proxy settings for their external communications.
proxy:
  httpProxy:
  httpsProxy:
  noProxy: 127.0.0.1,localhost,.local,.internal
  components:
    - core
    - jobservice
    - trivy
  • Allocate sufficient bandwidth: Ensure your Kubernetes cluster’s underlying network infrastructure and nodes have sufficient bandwidth to handle peak image pushes and pulls. Monitor network I/O on nodes running Harbor pods.

Conclusion

By diligently addressing these considerations, you can transform your basic Harbor deployment into a robust, secure, and highly available production-ready container registry. This approach ensures that Harbor serves as a cornerstone of your cloud-native infrastructure, capable of supporting demanding development and production workflows. From implementing High Availability and stringent security measures to optimizing storage and establishing proactive monitoring, each step contributes to a resilient and efficient artifact management system. 

Continue reading the Harbor Blog Series on cncf.io:

Blog 1 – Harbor: Enterprise-grade container registry for modern private cloud

Blog 2 – Deploying Harbor on Kubernetes using Helm

】

Harbor 是一个开源容器注册表，它通过策略和基于角色的访问控制来保护工件，确保扫描映像是否存在漏洞并将其签名为可信。要了解有关 Harbor 以及如何将其部署在虚拟机 (VM) 和 Kubernetes (K8s) 上的更多信息，请参阅部分 1 和 系列中的 2。

虽然部署 Harbor 很简单，但要使其投入生产需要仔细考虑几个关键方面。本博客概述了确保您的 Harbor 实例在生产环境中稳健、安全且可扩展的关键因素。

在本博客中，我们将重点关注通过 Helm 部署在 Kubernetes 上的 Harbor 作为基础，并为此具体部署提供建议。

1.高可用性 (HA) 和可扩展性

对于生产环境，单点故障是不可接受的，特别是对于将充当中央存储库的映像注册表，用于存储和拉取开发和生产应用程序的映像和工件。因此，实现 Harbor 的高可用性至关重要，涉及几个关键考虑因素：

  • 使用 Ingress 进行部署：在 Harbor 实例前面配置 Ingress 控制器类型的 Kubernetes 服务（例如 Traefik），以有效地分发传入流量，并提供统一的入口点以及用于证书管理的证书管理器。您可以在values.yaml 文件中指定这一点：
公开：
  类型：入口
  :
    启用：真
    证书来源：秘密 
  入口：
    主持人：
      核心：harbor.yourdomain.com
    注释：
      # 指定你的入口类
      kubernetes.io/ingress.class：traefik
      # 引用您的 ClusterIssuer（例如，自签名或内部 CA）
      cert-manager.io/cluster-issuer：“harbor-cluster-issuer”

要找到您的values.yaml文件，请参阅上一篇博客。

  • 利用多个 Harbor 实例：增加 value.yaml 中关键 Harbor 组件（例如 core、jobservice、portal、registry、trivy）的副本数量，以确保冗余。
核心：
  副本：3
就业服务：
  副本：3
门户网站：
  副本：3
注册表：
  副本：3
琐碎：
  副本：3

# 虽然不严格用于注册表本身的 HA，但请考虑增加导出器副本以实现强大的监控可用性
出口商：
  副本：3

# 或者，如果使用 Ingress，请考虑增加 Nginx 副本以提高 Ingress 可用性
nginx：
  副本：3

配置共享存储：对于持久数据，配置 Kubernetes StorageClasses 和 PersistentVolume 以使用 vSAN 或分布式文件系统等共享存储解决方案。在您的values.yaml中指定这些：

持久性：
  启用：真
  资源策略：“保留”
  持久卷声明：
    注册表：
      #如果留空，将使用kubernetes集群默认存储类
      storageClass：“您的存储类别”
     就业服务：
       storageClass：“您的存储类别”
     数据库：
       storageClass：“您的存储类别”
    雷迪斯：
      storageClass：“您的存储类别”
    琐碎：
      storageClass：“您的存储类别”
  • 启用数据库 HA (PostgreSQL)：虽然 Harbor 附带内置 PostgreSQL 数据库，但不建议用于生产用途，因为它：
  • 缺乏高可用性 (HA)：Harbor Helm 图表中的默认内部 PostgreSQL 设置通常是单个实例。这会产生单点故障。如果该数据库 Pod 出现故障，您的整个 Harbor 实例将不可用。
    1. 可扩展性有限：嵌入式数据库并非专为独立扩展而设计。如果您的 Harbor 使用量增加，您可能会遇到数据库性能瓶颈，而在不破坏 Harbor 本身的情况下很难解决该瓶颈。
    1. 复杂的生命周期管理：直接管理有状态数据库的备份、时间点恢复、修补和升级与专用数据库解决方案相比，应用程序 Helm 图表中的数据可能更加复杂且容易出错。

    因此，建议在 Kubernetes 内部署高度可用的 PostgreSQL 集群（例如，使用 Patroni 或 CloudNativePG 的 Helm 图表）或利用集群外部的托管数据库服务。通过更新values.yaml将Harbor配置为连接到此HA数据库：

    数据库：
  类型：“外部”
  外部：
    主机：“192.168.0.1”
    端口：“5432”
    用户名：“用户”
    密码：“密码”
    核心数据库：“注册表”
    # 如果使用现有的密钥，密钥必须是“password”
    现有秘密：“”
    #“禁用”-无 SSL
    # "require" - 始终 SSL（跳过验证）
    # "verify-ca" - 始终 SSL（验证由
    # 服务器由受信任的 CA 签名）
    #“verify-full”-始终 SSL（验证由
    # 服务器由受信任的 CA 签名，并且服务器主机名与之匹配
    # 在证书中）
    sslmode：“验证完整”

    实施 Redis HA：在 Kubernetes 中部署高度可用的 Redis 集群（例如，使用 Redis Sentinel 或 Redis 集群的 Helm 图表）或使用托管的 Redis 服务。通过更新 value.yaml 中的 redis.type 和连接详细信息，将 Harbor 配置为连接到此 HA Redis 实例。

    redis:
  类型： 外部
  外部：
    地址：“192.168.0.2:6397”
    哨兵大师设置：“”
    tls选项：
      启用：真
    用户名：“”
    密码：“”

    2.安全最佳实践

    安全性对于任何生产系统来说都是至关重要的，尤其是容器注册表。

    启用 TLS/SSL：始终为所有 Harbor 组件启用 TLS/SSL。

    公开：
  :
    启用：真
    certSource: auto # 如果使用 cert-manager，则更改为手动
    自动：
      通用名称：“”
内部TLS：
  启用：真
  Strong_ssl_ciphers：正确
  证书来源：“自动”
  核心：
    秘密名称：“”
  工作服务：
    秘密名称：“”
  注册表：
    秘密名称：“”
  门户网站：
    秘密名称：“”
  琐碎：
    秘密名称：“”


    配置身份验证和授权：利用 Harbor 支持的身份验证和授权机制来管理对 Harbor 资源的访问。 Harbor 部署完成后，按照 Harbor 配置指南将 Harbor 与 LDAP 或 OIDC 等企业身份提供商集成：配置 LDAP/Active Directory 身份验证 或 配置 OIDC 提供商身份验证。

    实施漏洞扫描：确保在values.yaml中启用漏洞扫描。 Harbor 默认使用 Trivy。在 Helm 图表中验证其激活和配置。

    琐事：
 启用：真

    激活内容信任：Harbor 支持多种内容信任机制，以确保您的工件的完整性。对于现代 OCI 工件签名，建议使用Cosign 和 Notation。在 Harbor UI 中或通过 Harbor API 在项目级别强制执行部署安全性，以仅允许部署经过验证的映像。这确保了只能部署受信任且经过加密签名的映像。

item.title: 【KubeCon + CloudNativeCon Europe 2026 Co-located Event Deep Dive: Open Source SecurityCon】KubeCon + CloudNativeCon Europe 2026 同期举办活动深入探讨：开源 SecurityCon
item.description:
【

Open Source SecurityCon (evolved from Cloud Native SecurityCon) returns for its second event, co-located with KubeCon + CloudNativeCon Europe 2026. The conference is dedicated to advancing innovation and collaboration across open source software security and cloud native security, bringing together creators, maintainers, operators, and consumers who are actively involved in securing the software ecosystem.

As open source continues to power modern infrastructure, the need to strengthen security practices across projects and production environments has never been more important. Open Source SecurityCon provides a focused space for the community to share insights, examine emerging challenges, and collectively improve the security posture of the cloud native ecosystem.

Who will get the most out of attending this event?

Open Source SecurityCon offers valuable insights for anyone, including those outside of a security practitioner role, who are involved in building, maintaining, or securing projects, platforms, or services. This includes open source maintainers, platform engineers, security practitioners, and operators running workloads in production.

Security is a shared responsibility across the entire software lifecycle—from development outputs to production deployments. Attendees will benefit from exploring ecosystem-wide challenges, identifying areas of weakness that may affect their own environments, and learning practical strategies to address them.

What is new and different this year?

This year’s program focuses on the latest advancements in security across policy, processes, and technology. The agenda spans topics from the implications of AI to foundational improvements in software supply chain security.

Attendees will gain insight into securing development outputs, strengthening existing systems, and addressing more complex, community-supported security challenges. The program reflects the evolving landscape of cloud native security and the growing importance of implementing secure practices at scale.

What will the day look like?

Open Source SecurityCon is designed as a single-stage event to encourage shared learning and broad participation. The day will feature a diverse lineup of deeply technical talks spanning topics from regulatory compliance to quantum cryptography, along with a high-profile panel and a series of lightning talks.

Sessions will explore advancements across security domains, offering perspectives that connect policy, engineering practices, and emerging technologies.

Should I do any homework first?

No specific prerequisites are required, but attendees are encouraged to approach security from a variety of perspectives, fueled by curiosity. Reviewing the schedule ahead of time can help you identify topics that genuinely interest you and see how they connect across different areas.

Security is often just one dimension of a broader technical focus. Thinking about how it intersects with your areas of interest will help you get the most out of the day.

Find your community!

Developing secure software in the open source domain at scale presents ongoing challenges, especially when feature development can move faster than security prioritization. Open Source SecurityCon brings the community together to highlight essential issues, share lessons learned, and foster collaboration across projects and organizations.

By creating space for open discussion and collective problem solving, the event helps strengthen the cloud native community and support the next wave of security leaders.

Don’t forget to register for KubeCon + CloudNativeCon Europe 2026.

】

Open Source SecurityCon（由 Cloud Native SecurityCon 演变而来）将举办第二届活动，与 KubeCon + CloudNativeCon Europe 2026 同期举办。该会议致力于推进开源软件安全和云原生安全领域的创新与协作，将积极参与保护软件生态系统的创建者、维护者、运营商和消费者聚集在一起。

随着开源继续为现代基础设施提供动力，加强跨项目和生产环境的安全实践的需求变得前所未有的重要。 Open Source SecurityCon 为社区提供了一个集中的空间来分享见解、研究新出现的挑战并共同改善云原生生态系统的安全状况。

谁将从参加本次活动中获益最多？

开源 SecurityCon 为任何参与构建、维护或保护项目、平台或服务的人（包括安全从业人员角色之外的人）提供了宝贵的见解。这包括开源维护人员、平台工程师、安全从业人员以及在生产中运行工作负载的操作员。

安全性是整个软件生命周期（从开发输出到生产部署）的共同责任。与会者将受益于探索整个生态系统的挑战、识别可能影响其自身环境的薄弱领域以及学习解决这些问题的实用策略。

今年有什么新的和不同的？

今年的计划重点关注政策、流程和技术方面的安全最新进展。议程涵盖的主题从人工智能的影响到软件供应链安全的根本性改进。

与会者将深入了解如何确保开发成果、加强现有系统以及解决更复杂、社区支持的安全挑战。该计划反映了云原生安全不断发展的格局以及大规模实施安全实践日益增长的重要性。

这一天会是什么样子？

Open Source SecurityCon 被设计为单阶段活动，以鼓励共享学习和广泛参与。这一天将举行一系列深入的技术演讲，涵盖从监管合规到量子加密等主题，以及高调的小组讨论和一系列闪电演讲。

会议将探讨安全领域的进步，提供将政策、工程实践和新兴技术联系起来的观点。

我应该先做作业吗？

没有特定的先决条件要求，但鼓励与会者在以下因素的推动下从各种角度探讨安全问题：好奇心。提前查看日程安排可以帮助您确定您真正感兴趣的主题，并了解它们在不同领域之间的联系。

安全性通常只是更广泛的技术焦点的一个方面。思考它如何与您感兴趣的领域相交叉将帮助您充分利用这一天。

找到您的社区！

在开源领域大规模开发安全软件面临着持续的挑战，特别是当功能开发速度快于安全优先级时。 Open Source SecurityCon 将社区聚集在一起，突出重要问题、分享经验教训并促进跨项目和组织的协作。

通过为公开讨论和集体问题解决创造空间，该活动有助于加强云原生社区并支持下一波安全领导者。

不要忘记注册参加 KubeCon + CloudNativeCon Europe 2026。

item.title: 【Kubernetes as AI’s operating system: 1.35 release signals】Kubernetes 作为 AI 操作系统：1.35 发布信号
item.description:
【

Why v1.35 reads like an AI-infrastructure release

Kubernetes has become the place where teams coordinate mixed production workloads: services, batch jobs, data pipelines, and ML training. The Kubernetes v1.35 (“Timbernetes”) release reinforces that trajectory with changes that reduce operational friction in scheduling, resource control, and configuration workflows.

What stands out in v1.35 is practical: fewer restarts for resizing, new primitives for coordinated placement, and safer defaults for how teams generate and review manifests at scale.

Taken together, these updates point to a Kubernetes control plane that’s adapting to bursty jobs, tightly coupled training runs, and continuously tuned inference services. Teams operating mixed clusters tend to feel the pressure first in placement efficiency, resize churn, and configuration review hygiene. The rest of this piece focuses on the v1.35 changes that ease those pressures and make AI/ML operations more predictable at scale.

The changes that matter for AI/ML operations

Workload-aware scheduling arrives (alpha)

Kubernetes v1.35 introduces the workload API and workload-aware scheduling, along with an initial implementation of gang scheduling for “all-or-nothing” placement across a group of Pods. This helps distributed training and tightly coupled jobs avoid partial placement patterns that waste capacity and stall progress.

If you want the deeper design context, the upstream proposal lives in the gang scheduling KEP.

In-place Pod resize is stable

v1.35 graduates in-place Pod resource resize to Stable. CPU and memory adjustments can happen without restarting containers, which reduces churn in inference services that need fast tuning under load and improves recovery options for long-running workloads.

The upstream spec is captured in the in-place update of Pod resources KEP.

Device allocation keeps moving toward a baseline capability

Dynamic Resource Allocation (DRA) is treated as a core building block for device-aware orchestration, and v1.35 keeps DRA enabled as part of the platform’s ongoing work in this area (see the DRA section in the v1.35 release notes). For AI/ML teams, the release continues ongoing efforts towards more predictable device claims and a cleaner path to richer scheduling semantics over accelerators.

KYAML becomes the default kubectl output format

Kubernetes is also tightening the “last mile” of configuration workflows. With v1.35, kubectl output defaults to KYAML, a stricter subset designed to reduce ambiguous YAML behaviors and common formatting hazards. The canonical description is in the KYAML reference.

If you need compatibility testing or controlled rollouts, the upstream design and toggles are documented in the KYAML KEP (including KUBECTL_KYAML=false).

Why AI keeps pushing teams toward a shared operating layer
